title: DPIA Worked Examples
description: Illustrative DPIA summaries for common Malaysian processing scenarios

examples.title: E-KYC Digital Onboarding
examples.scenario: A Malaysian bank implements e-KYC (electronic Know Your Customer) using facial recognition and MyKad (IC) OCR to verify customer identity during online account opening. Processing includes capturing selfie photos, extracting IC data, performing liveness detection, and matching against National Registration Department records.

risksIdentified.risk: Biometric data breach exposing facial templates
False rejection discriminating against certain demographics
Facial data retained beyond verification purpose
Unauthorized access to IC numbers in transit

mitigations: Biometric templates encrypted with AES-256 and stored separately from identity data
Facial matching algorithm tested across demographic groups with bias threshold <2%
Raw biometric data deleted within 24 hours of verification; only match result retained
End-to-end TLS 1.3 encryption for all data in transit
BNM RMiT-aligned access controls with MFA for staff accessing biometric systems
examples.residualRisk: Medium — acceptable with controls in place
examples.decision: Approved with quarterly review and annual bias audit

examples.title: Employee Monitoring System
examples.scenario: A Malaysian company with 2,000 employees deploys endpoint monitoring software on company-issued laptops. The system captures screenshots every 10 minutes, logs application usage, monitors email metadata, and tracks productivity metrics. Data is processed locally and sent to a cloud dashboard for managers.

risksIdentified.risk: Screenshots capture personal content (banking, health portals)
Productivity scoring creates discriminatory employment decisions
Excessive monitoring violates reasonable expectation of privacy
Cloud storage of screenshots exposes data to cross-border transfer

mitigations: Replace continuous screenshots with application-category logging (no visual capture)
Productivity metrics aggregated to team level; individual scores visible only to direct manager and HR
Clear employee notice (s7) with opt-out for personal device usage periods
Cloud dashboard hosted in Malaysian data center (no cross-border transfer)
90-day retention limit with automatic deletion of raw logs
examples.residualRisk: Medium — reduced from High after removing screenshot capture
examples.decision: Approved with modified scope (no screenshots) and 6-month review

examples.title: Customer Loyalty Program with Analytics
examples.scenario: A Malaysian retail chain launches a loyalty program across 200 stores. The program collects purchase history, location data from the mobile app, demographic information, and browsing behavior on the e-commerce platform. An AI model segments customers into 50 behavioral clusters for targeted marketing and dynamic pricing.

risksIdentified.risk: Location tracking reveals sensitive places (clinics, religious sites)
Dynamic pricing discriminates based on purchasing power or demographics
Customer profiling enables unwanted inference of sensitive attributes
Consent scope creep — data collected for loyalty used for unrelated purposes

mitigations: Location data aggregated to district level; no precise GPS coordinates stored
Dynamic pricing algorithm prohibited from using demographic attributes; pricing based on product category only
Granular consent with separate opt-ins for: loyalty points, marketing, location services, analytics
Data subject access portal for members to view, export, and delete their profile
Annual algorithm fairness audit by independent third party
examples.residualRisk: Low — acceptable with granular consent and location aggregation
examples.decision: Approved with annual review and fairness audit requirement

examples.title: Telemedicine Platform
examples.scenario: A Malaysian healthtech startup launches a telemedicine app connecting patients with licensed doctors. The platform processes patient registration data, medical history, consultation notes (text/video), prescriptions, and payment information. Video consultations are recorded for quality assurance. Data is stored on AWS Singapore with backup in AWS Sydney.

risksIdentified.risk: Medical data breach exposing health conditions and prescriptions
Video recordings captured without adequate informed consent
Cross-border backup to Australia without adequate protection assessment
Minors accessing platform without parental consent

mitigations: End-to-end encryption for all medical data; AES-256 at rest, TLS 1.3 in transit
Explicit opt-in consent for video recording with clear notice per consultation; recordings deleted after 90 days
Cross-border transfer agreement with AWS under s129; encryption keys held in Malaysia
Age verification gate requiring MyKad-based verification; under-18 requires parent/guardian consent flow
Medical data pseudonymized for analytics; k-anonymity threshold of 5 applied
examples.residualRisk: Medium — acceptable given healthcare benefit and controls
examples.decision: Approved with quarterly security review, annual penetration test, and MOH compliance check
